primary_diagnosis.name: Acute compartment syndrome of the lower leg (anterior compartment) following tibial fracture and rigid casting

comorbidities.name: Closed fracture of shaft of tibia (sustained earlier today)

pathophysiology_summary: A closed tibial fracture bleeds and the injured muscle swells within the fixed-volume fascial compartments of the lower leg. A rigid circumferential fiberglass cast prevents the compartment from expanding, so intracompartmental pressure rises above the capillary perfusion pressure. Blood flow to muscle and nerve falls, producing ischaemia: pain out of proportion to the injury, exquisite pain on passive stretch of the ischaemic muscles (moving the toes stretches the anterior compartment), then early nerve ischaemia (paraesthesia over the deep peroneal nerve distribution on the dorsum of the foot) and paresis. Distal arterial pulses are preserved until the very late stage because arterial pressure exceeds compartment pressure until decompensation — so palpable pulses do NOT exclude the diagnosis. Untreated, muscle and nerve undergo irreversible necrosis within hours, causing permanent contracture, disability or limb loss. The definitive treatment is emergency decompression: immediate removal/bivalving of the cast and urgent surgical fasciotomy.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly escalating pain out of proportion, refractory to strong opioids, hours after a rigid cast for a tibial fracture — the classic setup.

The dangerous anchor: attributing the pain to the known fracture and treating with more analgesia would miss the emergency. Distinguished by disproportion, opioid-refractoriness and pain on passive stretch.

differential.diagnosis: cast too tight / pressure effect without ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: A tight cast can cause pain, but does not by itself explain passive-stretch pain, paraesthesia and paresis.
Immobilisation and fracture are DVT risk factors, but the hyperacute onset (hours), the extreme pain and the nerve involvement fit ischaemia, not DVT.
Also limb-threatening, but primary arterial occlusion typically loses distal pulses immediately, whereas here pulses persist.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the escalating disproportionate pain after casting should already put compartment syndrome at the top and trigger red-flag screening — not a request to increase analgesia.

differential.evidence_basis: Pain out of proportion + opioid-refractory + pain on passive stretch + dorsal-foot paraesthesia + early toe paresis + tight shiny pale limb under a rigid cast — a coherent ischaemic constellation.
Excluded by disproportion, complete opioid-refractoriness, passive-stretch pain and new neurological signs.
The hours-long hyperacute timeline and severe pain with nerve involvement do not fit the slower onset of DVT.
Distal pulses remain palpable, arguing against primary arterial occlusion; but note pulses do NOT exclude compartment syndrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct move after the red-flag screen is emergency escalation for decompression, NOT further history-taking or analgesia titration.

differential.diagnosis: acute compartment syndrome of the leg (surgical emergency)
differential.evidence_basis: Time-critical limb-threatening diagnosis requiring immediate cast removal and urgent fasciotomy; confirmatory intracompartmental pressure measurement is a hospital investigation, not a telehealth one.

red_flags.description: Pain out of proportion to the injury and completely refractory to a strong opioid
red_flags.clinical_significance: The earliest and most sensitive sign of compartment ischaemia; opioid-refractory disproportionate pain after a fracture/cast is compartment syndrome until proven otherwise.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributed to under-treated fracture pain and managed with more analgesia, delaying decompression and allowing irreversible muscle and nerve necrosis.

red_flags.description: Excruciating pain on passive stretch (movement of the toes)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain on passive stretch of the muscles within the affected compartment is a hallmark clinical sign of compartment syndrome.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A key discriminating sign from ordinary fracture pain is lost and the emergency is under-recognised.

red_flags.description: New paraesthesia over the dorsum of the foot (deep peroneal nerve territory)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Early nerve ischaemia — a time-critical sign that the compartment pressure is already compromising neural tissue.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to permanent sensory loss and, with paresis, established nerve infarction.

red_flags.description: Tight, shiny, swollen, pale limb segment under a rigid circumferential cast
red_flags.clinical_significance: A rigid cast acting as a tourniquet over a swelling compartment — a mechanical driver that must be removed emergently.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The compartment continues to be constricted, accelerating ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Early paresis — weakness of toe movement developing on top of the pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Motor weakness is a later, ominous sign indicating advancing ischaemia of muscle and nerve.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Signals a narrowing window before irreversible loss; missing it risks permanent contracture (Volkmann) and disability.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the escalating pain to the known tibial fracture and treating it as ordinary post-injury pain, missing that its disproportion and opioid-refractoriness are the red flag.
Responding to opioid-refractory pain by recommending more analgesia — this masks the cardinal sign and delays the only effective treatment (decompression).
Using palpable distal pulses to exclude the diagnosis. In compartment syndrome pulses persist until the very late stage; their presence is falsely reassuring.
Advising the limb be elevated above the heart (routine swelling advice) — this lowers perfusion pressure and worsens ischaemia. The correct advice is to keep the limb level with the body.
Attempting to manage at home or advising a wait-and-see review rather than immediate escalation for cast removal and surgical assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Intracompartmental pressure by needle manometry (the confirmatory hospital test, expected >30 mmHg)
Palpation of a tense, woody compartment
Capillary refill time in the toes measured reliably
Objective grading of motor power and sensory deficit
Blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance to an emergency department with orthopaedic surgical capability for emergency cast removal/bivalving and urgent fasciotomy; while waiting, keep the limb level with the body (do NOT elevate above the heart), keep the toes as still as possible, do not walk on it, and do not apply ice or take further opioids to chase the pain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action, not over-diagnosis: dismissing the picture as ordinary fracture pain because sensation and pulses are partly intact, and titrating analgesia instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AO Surgery / RACS — acute compartment syndrome
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch are the earliest signs; suspicion mandates immediate removal of constricting casts and urgent fasciotomy — decompression within hours prevents irreversible necrosis.
